intro: Azadirachta indica balm, also known as neem balm, is a topical application made from the extracted oil of the neem tree (Azadirachta indica). This balm is often used to treat various skin issues, such as acne, eczema, and wounds, due to its antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and antibacterial properties. Additionally, neem balm is also known to have antifungal properties, making it effective in treating fungal infections and promoting overall skin health.
side_effects: Azadirachta indica balm can have side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, and potential interactions with medications, especially blood thinners and diabetes medications. Some users may experience digestive issues or dizziness after applying the balm. However, individual reactions can vary greatly depending on the concentration and individual tolerance to the balm's active ingredients.
uses: Azadirachta indica balm is used to treat various skin issues, including acne, eczema, and psoriasis, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. heavy_periods: Azadirachta indica balm, also known as neem balm, has been traditionally used to help alleviate heavy menstrual periods due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. fever: Azadirachta indica balm, also known as neem balm, is a traditional remedy used to alleviate fever and other symptoms of infections. The antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties of neem balm may help reduce the severity of fever and promote overall recovery.
recipe: To make azadirachta indica balm for medicinal use, first, infuse 1 part of dried Azadirachta indica leaves in 5 parts of carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, in a double boiler for 2-3 hours. Then, strain the mixture and combine it with 2-3 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 1 teaspoon of vitamin E oil. Finally, heat the mixture in a double boiler until the beeswax melts and the balm reaches a smooth, consistent texture, allowing it to cool before storing in airtight containers.
taste: Azadirachta indica balm is not typically consumed as a food or drink item, but some herbal teas or supplements may include it as a base. However, when used as a topical application, it doesn't have a direct taste effect on the senses. The balm's properties are usually applied to the skin for medicinal or therapeutic purposes.
